name: pyspry
documentation:
# A Springy Little Configuration Reader [![latest release](https://gitlab.com/bfosi/pyspry/-/badges/release.svg)](https://gitlab.com/bfosi/pyspry/-/releases) [![pipeline status](https://gitlab.com/bfosi/pyspry/badges/main/pipeline.svg)](https://gitlab.com/bfosi/pyspry/-/pipelines/latest) [![coverage report](https://gitlab.com/bfosi/pyspry/badges/main/coverage.svg)](https://bfosi.gitlab.io/pyspry/reports/pytest-html) [![Maintainability](https://api.codeclimate.com/v1/badges/996a01b1ab2df27571d5/maintainability)](https://codeclimate.com/github/bryant-finney/pyspry/maintainability) [![pylint](https://bfosi.gitlab.io/pyspry/reports/pylint.svg)](https://bfosi.gitlab.io/pyspry/reports/pylint-gitlab.html) [![PyPI version](https://badge.fury.io/py/pyspry.svg)](https://badge.fury.io/py/pyspry) [![Downloads](https://static.pepy.tech/badge/pyspry)](https://pepy.tech/project/pyspry) [![Code style: black](https://img.shields.io/badge/code%20style-black-000000.svg)](https://github.com/psf/black) [![docs: pdoc](https://img.shields.io/badge/docs-pdoc-blueviolet?logo=gitlab)](https://bfosi.gitlab.io/pyspry/pyspry.html) [![Checked with mypy](https://www.mypy-lang.org/static/mypy_badge.svg)](https://bfosi.gitlab.io/pyspry/reports/mypy-html) [![pre-commit.ci status](https://results.pre-commit.ci/badge/github/bryant-finney/pyspry/main.svg)](https://results.pre-commit.ci/latest/github/bryant-finney/pyspry/main) Influenced by [Spring Boot's YAML configuration features](https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/docs/1.1.0.M1/reference/html/boot-features-external-config.html#boot-features-external-config-yaml), this library reads system configuration settings from environment variables and YAML files. ## Installation To install using `pip`: ```sh pip install pyspry ``` ## Usage By default, a file named `config.yml` in the current directory will be loaded and parsed for configuration settings. These can be accessed through the `pyspry.settings` module. For example, consider the following `config.yml` file: 
DATABASES:
  default:
    AUTOCOMMIT: true
    NAME: db.sqlite3
DEBUG: true
DEFAULT_CHARSET: utf-8
These configuration settings can be accessed as follows: ```py >>> from pyspry import settings >>> settings.DEBUG True >>> settings.DEFAULT_CHARSET 'utf-8' ``` ### Specifying Alternate Config Files Set the environment variable `PYSPRY_CONFIG_PATH` to override the default path to the configuration file: ```py >>> import os; os.environ["PYSPRY_CONFIG_PATH"] 'sample-config.yml' >>> from pyspry import settings >>> settings.PYSPRY_STATICFILES_FINDERS ['django.contrib.staticfiles.finders.FileSystemFinder', 'django.contrib.staticfiles.finders.AppDirectoriesFinder'] ``` ### Variable Prefixes Set the environment variable `PYSPRY_VAR_PREFIX` to filter which settings are loaded: ```py >>> import os; os.environ["PYSPRY_VAR_PREFIX"] 'PYSPRY' >>> from pyspry import settings >>> "TEST_RUNNER" in settings # the prefix is automatically inserted True >>> "IGNORED_SETTING" in settings # see the last line in 'sample-config.yml' False ``` ### Nested Structures If the configuration includes nested data structures, each layer of nesting can be traversed using `_`-separated names: ```py >>> settings.LOGGING["version"] == settings.LOGGING_version == 1 True >>> settings.LOGGING["loggers"]["pyspry"]["level"] == \ ... settings.LOGGING_loggers_pyspry_level == 'DEBUG' True ``` ### Environment Variables In many cases, it can be useful to set one-off overrides for a setting. This can be done with an environment variable: ```py >>> import importlib, os >>> settings.LOGGING_loggers_pyspry_level 'DEBUG' >>> os.environ["PYSPRY_LOGGING_loggers_pyspry_level"] = "INFO" >>> settings = importlib.reload(settings) >>> settings.LOGGING["loggers"]["pyspry"]["level"] 'INFO' ``` ### Django Integration This package was originally designed for use with the [Django](https://www.djangoproject.com/) framework. To use it: ```sh # after installing the package, specify it as the settings module export DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE=pyspry.settings django-admin diffsettings ``` ## Development The following system dependencies are required: - [`poetry`](https://python-poetry.org/docs/#installation) - [`pre-commit`](https://pre-commit.com/#install) - (optional) [`direnv`](https://direnv.net/docs/installation.html) - (optional) [`docker`](https://docs.docker.com/get-docker/) Common development commands are managed by [`poethepoet`](https://github.com/nat-n/poethepoet); run `poe --help` for an up-to-date list of commands: ```txt Poe the Poet - A task runner that works well with poetry. version 0.20.0 USAGE poe [-h] [-v | -q] [--root PATH] [--ansi | --no-ansi] task [task arguments] GLOBAL OPTIONS -h, --help Show this help page and exit --version Print the version and exit -v, --verbose Increase command output (repeatable) -q, --quiet Decrease command output (repeatable) -d, --dry-run Print the task contents but don't actually run it --root PATH Specify where to find the pyproject.toml --ansi Force enable ANSI output --no-ansi Force disable ANSI output CONFIGURED TASKS setup-versioning Install the 'poetry-dynamic-versioning' plugin to the local 'poetry' installation docs Generate this package's docs docserver Use 'pdoc' to launch an HTTP server for this package's docs lab Run Jupyter Lab lint Lint this package test Test this package and report coverage ``` ## Reports - [`bandit`](https://bfosi.gitlab.io/pyspry/reports/bandit.html) - [`mypy`](https://bfosi.gitlab.io/pyspry/reports/mypy-html/index.html) - [`pytest` coverage](https://bfosi.gitlab.io/pyspry/reports/pytest-html/index.html)

modules.name: pyspry.keysview
documentation: Define a `KeysView` class based on the current Python interpreter version.

modules.name: pyspry.keysview.py38plus
documentation: Override `typing.KeysView` for Python >3.8.

variables.name: pyspry.keysview.py38plus.KT
documentation: A typing.TypeVar type to represent dictionary keys.

classes.name: pyspry.keysview.py38plus.NestedKeysView

methods.name: pyspry.keysview.py38plus.NestedKeysView.__init__
documentation:
Prepend `prefix` to each key in the view, with a `sep` delimiter.

Args:
    mapping (typing.Mapping[KT, typing.Any]): create a view of this
        mapping object's keys
    prefix (builtins.str): prepend this string to each key in the view; defaults to
        an empty string
    sep (builtins.str): join each layer of nested keys with this separator; defaults to
        `_`

methods.name: pyspry.keysview.py38plus.NestedKeysView.__iter__
documentation: Override the parent class to return a string matching layers of nesting.
pylint: disable=duplicate-code

modules.name: pyspry.keysview.py38
documentation: Override the `collections.abc.KeysView` class for Python 3.8.

methods.name: pyspry.keysview.py38.NestedKeysView.__init__
documentation:
Prepend `prefix` to each key in the view, with a `sep` delimiter.

Args:
    mapping (Mapping[KT, Any]): create a view of this mapping object's keys
    prefix (str): prepend this string to each key in the view; defaults to ""
    sep (str): join each layer of nested keys with this separator; defaults to "_".

methods.name: pyspry.keysview.py38.NestedKeysView.__iter__
documentation: Override the parent class to return a string matching layers of nesting.

documentation: type: ignore[type-arg]

```
.. include:: ../../README.md
```

modules.name: pyspry.settings
documentation:
Bootstrap this module with a `Settings` object created from the `PYSPRY_CONFIG_PATH` file.

To update the settings in this module, open the default YAML file path and change the settings
there.

modules.name: pyspry.base
documentation: Define the base `Settings` class.

classes.name: pyspry.base.NullMeta

methods.name: pyspry.base.NullMeta.__eq__
documentation: Check `cls` for equivalence, as well as `None`.

methods.name: pyspry.base.NullMeta.__getattr__
documentation:
Unless `__name` starts with `_`, return the `NullMeta` class instance.

The check for a `_` prefix allows Python's internal mechanics (such as the `__dict__`
or `__doc__` attributes) to function correctly.

methods.name: pyspry.base.NullMeta.__new__
documentation: Create new `class` instances from this `metaclass`.

class_variables.name: pyspry.base.NullMeta.__or__
documentation: type: ignore[assignment]
Classes using this `metaclass` return themselves for every operation / interaction.

classes.name: pyspry.base.Null
documentation:
Define a class which returns itself for all interactions.

```pycon
>>> Null == None, Null is None
(True, False)
```

```pycon
>>> for result in [
...     Null(),
...     Null[0],
...     Null["any-key"],
...     Null.any_attr,
...     Null().any_attr,
...     Null + 5,
...     Null - 5,
...     Null * 5,
...     Null / 5,
...     Null % 5,
...     5 + Null,
...     5 - Null,
...     5 * Null,
...     5 / Null,
...     5 % Null,
... ]:
...     assert result is Null, result
```

```pycon
>>> str(Null)
'Null'
```

```pycon
>>> bool(Null)
False
```

Null is always false-y:

```pycon
>>> Null or "None"
'None'
```

classes.name: pyspry.base.ModuleContainer

class_variables.name: pyspry.base.ModuleContainer.name
documentation: Absolute import path of the module, e.g. `pyspry.settings`.

class_variables.name: pyspry.base.ModuleContainer.module
documentation: The module pulled from `sys.modules`, or `None` if it hadn't already been imported.
Pair the instance of a module with its name.

classes.name: pyspry.base.Settings

methods.name: pyspry.base.Settings.__init__
documentation:
Deserialize all JSON-encoded environment variables during initialization.

Args:

: config (builtins.dict[builtins.str, typing.Any]): the values loaded from a JSON/YAML

  : file

  environ (builtins.dict[builtins.str, typing.Any]): override config settings with these

  : environment variables

  prefix (builtins.str): insert / strip this prefix when needed

The `prefix` is automatically added when accessing attributes:

```pycon
>>> settings = Settings({"APP_NAME_EXAMPLE_PARAM": 0}, {}, prefix="APP_NAME")
>>> settings.APP_NAME_EXAMPLE_PARAM == settings.EXAMPLE_PARAM == 0
True
```

methods.name: pyspry.base.Settings.__contains__
documentation:
Check the merged `NestedDict` config for a setting with the given name.

Keys must be strings to avoid unexpected behavior.

```pycon
>>> settings = Settings({20: "oops", "20": "okay"}, environ={}, prefix="")
>>> "20" in settings
True
>>> 20 in settings
False
```

methods.name: pyspry.base.Settings.__dir__
documentation: Return a set of the names of all settings provided by this object.

methods.name: pyspry.base.Settings.__getattr__
documentation:
Prioritize retrieving values from environment variables, falling back to the file config.

Args:
    name (str): the name of the setting to retrieve

Returns:
    `Any`: the value of the setting

methods.name: pyspry.base.Settings.bootstrap
documentation:
Store the named module object, replacing it with `self` to bootstrap the import mechanic.

This object will replace the named module in `sys.modules`.

Args:
    module_name (builtins.str): the name of the module to replace

Returns:
    typing.Optional[types.ModuleType]: the module object that was replaced, or `None` if the
        module wasn't already in `sys.modules`

methods.name: pyspry.base.Settings.load
documentation:
Load the specified configuration file and environment variables.

Args:
    file_path (pathlib.Path | builtins.str): the path to the config file to load
    prefix (typing.Optional[builtins.str]): if provided, parse all env variables containing
        this prefix

Returns:
    pyspry.base.Settings: the `Settings` object loaded from file with environment variable
        overrides

methods.name: pyspry.base.Settings.maybe_add_prefix
documentation:
If the given name is missing the prefix configured for these settings, insert it.

Args:
    name (builtins.str): the attribute / key name to massage

Returns:
    builtins.str: the name with the prefix inserted `iff` the prefix was missing

methods.name: pyspry.base.Settings.restore
documentation:
Remove `self` from `sys.modules` and restore the module that was bootstrapped.

When a module is bootstrapped, it is replaced by a `Settings` object:

```pycon
>>> type(sys.modules["pyspry.settings"])
<class 'pyspry.base.Settings'>
```

Calling this method reverts the bootstrapping:

```pycon
>>> mod = settings.restore()
>>> type(sys.modules["pyspry.settings"])
<class 'module'>
```

```pycon
>>> mod is sys.modules["pyspry.settings"]
True
```

class_variables.name: pyspry.base.Settings.__config
documentation: Store the config file contents as a `NestedDict` object.

class_variables.name: pyspry.base.Settings.prefix
documentation: Only load settings whose names start with this prefix.

class_variables.name: pyspry.base.Settings.module_container
documentation:
This property is set by the `Settings.bootstrap()` method and removed by
`Settings.restore()`

Store settings from environment variables and a config file.

\# Usage

```pycon
>>> settings = Settings.load(config_path, prefix="APP_NAME")
>>> settings.APP_NAME_EXAMPLE_PARAM
'a string!'
```

\## Environment Variables

Monkeypatch an environment variable for this test:

```pycon
>>> getfixture("monkey_example_param")  # use an env var to override the above setting
{'APP_NAME_EXAMPLE_PARAM': 'monkeypatched!'}
```

Setting an environment variable (above) can override specific settings values:

```pycon
>>> settings = Settings.load(config_path, prefix="APP_NAME")
>>> settings.APP_NAME_EXAMPLE_PARAM
'monkeypatched!'
```

\## JSON Values

Environment variables in JSON format are parsed:

```pycon
>>> list(settings.APP_NAME_ATTR_A)
[1, 2, 3]
```

```pycon
>>> getfixture("monkey_attr_a")    # override an environment variable
{'APP_NAME_ATTR_A': '[4, 5, 6]'}
```

```pycon
>>> settings = Settings.load(config_path, prefix="APP_NAME")    # and reload the settings
>>> list(settings.APP_NAME_ATTR_A)
[4, 5, 6]
```

To list all settings, use the built-in `dir()` function:

```pycon
>>> dir(settings)
['ATTR_A', 'ATTR_A_0', 'ATTR_A_1', 'ATTR_A_2', 'ATTR_B', 'ATTR_B_K', 'EXAMPLE_PARAM']
```

modules.name: pyspry.nested_dict
documentation: Define a `NestedDict` class designed for nested configuration settings.

classes.name: pyspry.nested_dict.NestedDict

methods.name: pyspry.nested_dict.NestedDict.__init__
documentation: Similar to the `dict` signature, accept a single optional positional argument.

methods.name: pyspry.nested_dict.NestedDict.__contains__
documentation:
Check if `self.__data` provides the specified key.

Also consider nesting when evaluating the condition, i.e.

```pycon
>>> example = NestedDict({"KEY": {"SUB": {"NAME": "test"}}})
>>> "KEY_SUB" in example
True
>>> "KEY_SUB_NAME" in example
True
```

```pycon
>>> "KEY_MISSING" in example
False
```

methods.name: pyspry.nested_dict.NestedDict.__delitem__
documentation: Delete the object with the specified key from the internal data structure.

methods.name: pyspry.nested_dict.NestedDict.__getitem__
documentation: Traverse nesting according to the `NestedDict.sep` property.

methods.name: pyspry.nested_dict.NestedDict.__ior__
documentation:
Override settings in this object with settings from the specified object.

```pycon
>>> example = NestedDict({"KEY": {"SUB": {"NAME": "test", "OTHER": 99}}})
>>> example |= NestedDict({"KEY_SUB_NAME": "test2"})
>>> example.serialize()
{'KEY': {'SUB': {'NAME': 'test2', 'OTHER': 99}}}
```

```pycon
>>> example = NestedDict(["A", "B", "C"])
>>> example |= NestedDict(["D", "E"])
>>> example.serialize()
['D', 'E']
```

methods.name: pyspry.nested_dict.NestedDict.__iter__
documentation: Return an iterator from the internal data structure.

methods.name: pyspry.nested_dict.NestedDict.__len__
documentation: Proxy the `__len__` method of the `__data` attribute.

methods.name: pyspry.nested_dict.NestedDict.__or__
documentation:
Override the bitwise `or` operator to support merging `NestedDict` objects.

```pycon
>>> ( NestedDict({"A": {"B": 0}}) | {"A_B": 1} ).serialize()
{'A': {'B': 1}}
```

```pycon
>>> NestedDict({"A": 0}) | [0, 1]
Traceback (most recent call last):
...
TypeError: cannot merge [0, 1] (list: True) with NestedDict({'A': 0}) (list: False)
```

```pycon
>>> NestedDict([0, {"A": 1}]) | [1, {"B": 2}]
NestedDict({'0': 1, '1': NestedDict({'A': 1, 'B': 2})})
```

methods.name: pyspry.nested_dict.NestedDict.__repr__
documentation: Use a `str` representation similar to `dict`, but wrap it in the class name.

methods.name: pyspry.nested_dict.NestedDict.__ror__
documentation:
Cast the other object to a `NestedDict` when needed.

```pycon
>>> {"A": 0, "B": 1} | NestedDict({"A": 2})
NestedDict({'A': 2, 'B': 1})
```

```pycon
>>> merged_lists = ["A", "B", "C"]  | NestedDict([1, 2])
>>> merged_lists.serialize()
[1, 2]
```

methods.name: pyspry.nested_dict.NestedDict.__setitem__
documentation: Similar to `__getitem__`, traverse nesting at `NestedDict.sep` in the key.

methods.name: pyspry.nested_dict.NestedDict._reduce
documentation: Delete keys from `base` that are not present in `incoming`.

methods.name: pyspry.nested_dict.NestedDict.get_first_match
documentation:
Traverse nested settings to retrieve the value of `nested_name`.

Args:
    nested_name (builtins.str): the key to break across the nested data structure

Returns:
    `typing.Any`: the value retrieved from this object or a nested object

Raises:
    builtins.ValueError: `nested_name` does not correctly identify a key in this object
        or any of its child objects

methods.name: pyspry.nested_dict.NestedDict.is_list
documentation:
Return `True` if the internal data structure is a `list`.

```pycon
>>> NestedDict([1, 2, 3]).is_list
True
```

```pycon
>>> NestedDict({"A": 0}).is_list
False
```

methods.name: pyspry.nested_dict.NestedDict.keys
documentation:
Flatten the nested dictionary to collect the full list of keys.

```pycon
>>> example = NestedDict({"KEY": {"SUB": {"NAME": "test", "OTHER": 1}}})
>>> list(example.keys())
['KEY', 'KEY_SUB', 'KEY_SUB_NAME', 'KEY_SUB_OTHER']
```

methods.name: pyspry.nested_dict.NestedDict.maybe_merge
documentation:
If the given objects are both `typing.Mapping` subclasses, merge them.

Also check if the `target` object is an instance of this class. If it is, and if it's based
on a list, reduce the result to remove list elements that are not present in `incoming`.

```pycon
>>> example = NestedDict({"key": [1, 2, 3], "other": "val"})
>>> NestedDict.maybe_merge(NestedDict({"key": [4, 5]}), example)
True
>>> example.serialize()
{'key': [4, 5], 'other': 'val'}
```

Args:
: incoming (typing.Mapping[builtins.str, typing.Any] | typing.Any): test this object to

  : verify it is a `typing.Mapping`

  target (typing.MutableMapping[builtins.str, typing.Any]): update this

  : `typing.MutableMapping` with the `incoming` mapping

Returns:

: builtins.bool: the two `typing.Mapping` objects were merged

methods.name: pyspry.nested_dict.NestedDict.maybe_strip
documentation: Remove the specified prefix from the given string (if present).

methods.name: pyspry.nested_dict.NestedDict.serialize
documentation: Convert the `NestedDict` back to a `dict` or `list`.

methods.name: pyspry.nested_dict.NestedDict.squash
documentation:
Collapse all nested keys in the given dictionary.

```pycon
>>> sample = {"A": {"B": {"C": 0}, "B_D": 2}, "A_THING": True, "A_B_C": 1, "N_KEYS": 0}
>>> nested = NestedDict(sample)
>>> nested.squash()
>>> nested.serialize()
{'A': {'B': {'C': 1, 'D': 2}, 'THING': True}, 'N_KEYS': 0}
```

Traverse nested data structures.

\# Usage

```pycon
>>> d = NestedDict(
...     {
...         "PARAM_A": "a",
...         "PARAM_B": 0,
...         "SUB": {"A": 1, "B": ["1", "2", "3"]},
...         "list": [{"A": 0, "B": 1}, {"a": 0, "b": 1}],
...         "deeply": {"nested": {"dict": {"ionary": {"zero": 0}}}},
...         "strings": ["should", "also", "work"]
...     }
... )
```

Simple keys work just like standard dictionaries:

```pycon
>>> d["PARAM_A"], d["PARAM_B"]
('a', 0)
```

Nested containers are converted to `NestedDict` objects:

```pycon
>>> d["SUB"]
NestedDict({'A': 1, 'B': NestedDict({'0': '1', '1': '2', '2': '3'})})
```

```pycon
>>> d["SUB_B"]
NestedDict({'0': '1', '1': '2', '2': '3'})
```

Nested containers can be accessed by appending the nested key name to the parent key name:

```pycon
>>> d["SUB_A"] == d["SUB"]["A"]
True
```

```pycon
>>> d["SUB_A"]
1
```

```pycon
>>> d["deeply_nested_dict_ionary_zero"]
0
```

List indices can be accessed too:

```pycon
>>> d["SUB_B_0"], d["SUB_B_1"]
('1', '2')
```

Similarly, the `in` operator also traverses nesting:

```pycon
>>> "SUB_B_0" in d
True
```
